∑Big Book of Tech Exams

The Big Book of Technical Exams 📚

A concept-first study guide for DS/ML interviews and data-engineering exams, ordered like a real project: explore your data, build & evaluate models, prep for the interview, then grab ready-made code. It covers Python, SQL & ETL, a full matplotlib/seaborn visualisation gallery, math & statistics, machine learning, data structures & algorithms, interview prep, and a copy-paste-modify cargo cookbook — explained first, with code and formulas as illustration. Read it, don't just copy it.

🐍 Python🗄️ SQL & ETL🐼 Pandas📊 Visualisation📐 Math & Stats🤖 Machine Learning🧱 Data Structures🎯 Interview Prep🧰 Cargo Cookbook
How to use this book. Each topic opens with the idea — what it is and when you'd reach for it — before any syntax. Try to recall or re-derive the answer before reading the code or formula. Use the search bar (or press /) to jump anywhere; Esc clears it. Collapse sections you've mastered. Everything renders offline-friendly except the highlight/MathJax CDNs.
No matches. Try another term, or press Esc to clear.
🐍

Python

Python is dynamically typed and interpreted: names are just labels bound to objects, and an object carries its own type. Most of what you need for interviews is fluency with the built-in container types, comprehensions, and a handful of standard-library tools. The mental model to hold onto: everything is an object, variables are references, and "mutable vs immutable" decides whether an operation copies or modifies in place.

⚡ Cheat-sheet — quick recall

  1. Everything is an object; variables are references to objects.
  2. Mutable (list/dict/set) vs immutable (int/str/tuple) decides copy-vs-modify-in-place.
  3. Never use a mutable default arg — def f(x=[]) shares one list across calls.
  4. is tests identity, == tests value.
  5. Comprehensions beat manual loops; (…) generators are lazy & constant-memory.
  6. Scope is LEGB; global/nonlocal rebind outer names.
  7. Slicing a[i:j:k] is half-open and never errors out of range.
  8. dict/set lookups are O(1); list membership is O(n).
  9. Prefer enumerate/zip/f-strings over index bookkeeping.
  10. Use with for resources; favour EAFP (try/except) over pre-checking.

In this chapter

  • Core types & the object model
  • Numbers & operators
  • Strings
  • Lists
  • Tuples & sequences
  • Dictionaries
  • Sets
  • Comprehensions
  • Control flow
  • Functions & scope
  • Classes & OOP
  • Iterators & generators
  • Errors & context managers
  • Standard library toolbox
  • Built-in time complexity
  • Interview gotchas

Core types & the object model

Every value is an object with an identity (id()), a type (type()), and a value. The single most exam-relevant distinction is mutability: immutable objects (int, float, str, tuple, frozenset, bytes) can never change — "modifying" one actually creates a new object — while mutable objects (list, dict, set, bytearray) can be changed in place. This is why passing a list into a function can change the caller's list, but passing an int cannot.

TypeMutable?Ordered?Use it for
int, float, complex, boolNo—Numbers; bool is a subclass of int
strNoYesText, immutable sequence of characters
listYesYesOrdered, growable sequence; the default workhorse
tupleNoYesFixed records; hashable so usable as dict keys
dictYesInsertion-ordered (3.7+)Key→value lookups in O(1)
set / frozensetYes / NoNoMembership tests, dedup, set algebra
NoneType——The sentinel "no value", singleton None
Identity vs equality. == compares values; is compares identity (same object in memory). Use is only for singletons like None: write if x is None, never if x == None.
python
x = [1, 2, 3]
y = x              # y and x reference the SAME list
y.append(4)
print(x)           # [1, 2, 3, 4]  -- both changed
z = x.copy()       # shallow copy -> independent top level
import copy
deep = copy.deepcopy(nested)   # copies nested objects too

type(3)            # <class 'int'>
isinstance(3, int) # True  (prefer over type(x) == int)
id(x), id(y)       # same number; id(z) differs
Truthiness. Empty containers ([] {} () "" set()), 0, 0.0, and None are falsy; almost everything else is truthy. So if items: is the idiomatic "is this list non-empty?" check.

Numbers & operators

Integers are arbitrary precision (no overflow), so factorials and big sums just work. Division always has two flavours you must keep straight: / is true division returning a float, // is floor division. Floats are IEEE-754 doubles, so equality comparisons are unreliable — compare with a tolerance instead.

python
7 / 2      # 3.5   true division -> float
7 // 2     # 3     floor division
-7 // 2    # -4    floors toward negative infinity (not 0!)
7 % 3      # 1     modulo; sign follows the divisor in Python
2 ** 10    # 1024  exponent
divmod(7, 3)        # (2, 1)  quotient and remainder together

import math
math.isclose(0.1 + 0.2, 0.3)   # True  -- never use == on floats
round(2.675, 2)                # 2.67 (binary float surprise)
abs(-5); math.floor(2.9); math.ceil(2.1); math.sqrt(16)
float('inf'); float('nan')     # special values
Bit tricks for interviews. x & 1 tests odd/even, x << n multiplies by \(2^n\), x >> n divides, x & (x-1) clears the lowest set bit (count bits / check power of two), x ^ y finds the single non-duplicated value in a list.

Strings

Strings are immutable sequences, so every "edit" returns a new string. That immutability is why building a string in a loop with += is quadratic — accumulate into a list and "".join() at the end instead. f-strings are the modern way to format and also support a debug = form and format specs.

python
s = "Data Science"
s.lower(); s.upper(); s.title(); s.strip()
s.replace("Data", "Computer")
s.split()              # ['Data', 'Science']   split on whitespace
"a,b,c".split(",")     # ['a','b','c']
",".join(["a","b"])    # 'a,b'   <-- preferred way to build strings
s.startswith("Data"); s.endswith("ce"); "Sci" in s
s.find("Sci")          # 5  (-1 if absent); .index raises if absent
s[0]; s[-1]; s[:4]; s[::-1]   # indexing & slicing (reverse!)

name, score = "Ada", 0.97
f"{name} scored {score:.1%}"   # 'Ada scored 97.0%'
f"{score=}"                     # 'score=0.97'  debug form
"  pad ".rjust(8); "x".zfill(3)  # '00x'
ord('A'); chr(65)                # 65 ; 'A'
Common bug. str.replace/strip/sort… do not mutate — they return a new value. s.strip() alone does nothing; you must write s = s.strip().

Lists

A list is a dynamic array: indexing and appending are O(1) amortized, but inserting or deleting at the front is O(n) because everything shifts. Know which methods mutate in place (and return None) versus which return a new object — mixing this up is the most common silent bug in interview code.

OperationEffectCost
a.append(x)add to end (mutates)O(1)*
a.pop() / a.pop(0)remove last / firstO(1) / O(n)
a.insert(i, x)insert at indexO(n)
a.remove(x)delete first match by valueO(n)
a.sort() / sorted(a)in-place / new sorted listO(n log n)
x in amembership scanO(n)
a.reverse() / a[::-1]in-place / new reversedO(n)
python
a = [3, 1, 2]
a.append(4); a.extend([5, 6]); a += [7]   # extend != append
a.sort()                       # mutates, returns None
b = sorted(a, reverse=True)    # new list
a.sort(key=lambda x: -x)       # sort by a derived key
nums = [(1,'b'), (2,'a')]
nums.sort(key=lambda t: t[1])  # sort tuples by 2nd element

# Slicing copies; assignment to a slice mutates
a[1:3] = [99]                  # replace a range
first, *middle, last = [1,2,3,4]   # 1, [2,3], 4  (unpacking)

# DANGER: shared inner references
grid = [[0]*3]*3               # all 3 rows are the SAME list!
grid = [[0]*3 for _ in range(3)]   # correct independent rows
Mutable default arguments. def f(acc=[]): reuses the same list across calls. Use def f(acc=None): acc = acc or [] instead.

Tuples & sequence protocol

Tuples are immutable lists, which makes them hashable — that's why they (not lists) can be dictionary keys or set members. They're also the natural type for returning multiple values and for fixed records. Named tuples and dataclasses give you readable field access without writing boilerplate.

python
point = (3, 4)
x, y = point                    # unpacking
single = (5,)                   # one-element tuple needs the comma

from collections import namedtuple
Pt = namedtuple("Pt", "x y")
p = Pt(3, 4); p.x; p.y          # readable, still immutable

from dataclasses import dataclass
@dataclass
class User:
    name: str
    age: int = 0                # auto __init__, __repr__, __eq__

Dictionaries

The dict is Python's hash table and your go-to for O(1) lookups, counting, grouping, caching, and adjacency lists. Since 3.7 it preserves insertion order. The key skills: safe access that won't raise, and the counting/grouping idioms that appear in almost every data interview.

python
d = {"a": 1, "b": 2}
d["c"] = 3                      # insert / update
d.get("z", 0)                   # 0 if missing -- no KeyError
d.setdefault("a", 99)           # returns 1; only sets if absent
d.pop("a", None); del d["b"]
"a" in d                        # key membership, O(1)
d.keys(); d.values(); d.items() # views
for k, v in d.items(): ...
{**d1, **d2}                    # merge (d2 wins);  d1 | d2 in 3.9+

# Counting & grouping -- memorize these
from collections import Counter, defaultdict
Counter("mississippi").most_common(2)   # [('i',4),('s',4)]
groups = defaultdict(list)
for word in words:
    groups[len(word)].append(word)       # auto-creates the list

# Dict comprehension & inversion
squares = {n: n*n for n in range(5)}
inv = {v: k for k, v in d.items()}
Counter is a multiset. It supports + - & | between counters and .most_common(), making "top-k frequent" a two-liner.

Sets

Sets give O(1) membership and automatic deduplication, plus the algebra of union/intersection/difference. Whenever a problem says "have I seen this before?" or "what's common between these?", a set is usually the answer and often turns an O(n²) scan into O(n).

python
s = {1, 2, 3}; empty = set()    # {} is an empty DICT, not a set
s.add(4); s.discard(9)          # discard won't raise; remove will
2 in s                          # O(1) membership
a, b = {1,2,3}, {2,3,4}
a | b      # union {1,2,3,4}
a & b      # intersection {2,3}
a - b      # difference {1}
a ^ b      # symmetric difference {1,4}
a <= b     # subset test
unique = set(my_list)           # dedup in one call
frozenset({1,2})                # immutable -> hashable

Comprehensions & functional tools

Comprehensions are the Pythonic replacement for building a collection with a loop: they're faster and read as "what I want, for each item, where condition". The same syntax produces lists, sets, dicts, and (with parentheses) lazy generators. Pair them with map/filter/zip/enumerate and you can express most transforms in one line.

python
[x*x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]        # list
{x % 3 for x in range(10)}                    # set
{x: x*x for x in range(5)}                    # dict
(x*x for x in range(10**9))                   # generator: lazy, O(1) memory
[[r*c for c in range(3)] for r in range(3)]   # nested / 2D
[y for row in matrix for y in row]            # flatten (order = nested loops)
[a if a > 0 else 0 for a in vals]             # ternary inside

# Iteration helpers
for i, v in enumerate(seq, start=1): ...      # index + value
for a, b in zip(xs, ys): ...                  # parallel iterate
list(zip(*matrix))                            # transpose!
from itertools import product, combinations, permutations, accumulate, chain, groupby
list(combinations([1,2,3], 2))                # [(1,2),(1,3),(2,3)]
list(accumulate([1,2,3,4]))                   # running sum [1,3,6,10]

Control flow

Beyond the usual if/elif/else and loops, two features matter for clean exam code: the for…else clause (the else runs only if the loop finished without break — handy for search loops), and structural pattern matching (match/case, 3.10+) for dispatching on shape.

python
for n in nums:
    if n < 0:
        break
else:
    print("no negatives found")     # runs only if no break

while queue:
    node = queue.pop()
    ...

# match/case (3.10+): destructure by shape
match command.split():
    case ["go", direction]:   move(direction)
    case ["quit"]:            stop()
    case _:                   print("unknown")

value = "pos" if x > 0 else "neg"   # ternary expression

Functions, scope & arguments

Functions are first-class objects: you can pass them around, return them, and store them. Master the argument forms — positional, keyword, defaults, *args, **kwargs — and the LEGB scope rule (Local → Enclosing → Global → Built-in) that decides which name a lookup finds. Closures capture enclosing variables by reference, which is the basis of decorators.

python
def f(a, b=10, *args, key=None, **kwargs):
    # a: required, b: default, args: extra positionals (tuple),
    # key: keyword-only, kwargs: extra keywords (dict)
    return a + b

f(1, 2, 3, key="x", z=9)
nums = [1, 2, 3]
print(*nums)                 # unpack into positionals -> 1 2 3
f(**{"a": 1, "b": 2})        # unpack dict into keywords

# Closures & decorators
def timer(fn):
    import functools, time
    @functools.wraps(fn)
    def wrapper(*a, **k):
        t = time.perf_counter(); r = fn(*a, **k)
        print(f"{fn.__name__}: {time.perf_counter()-t:.4f}s")
        return r
    return wrapper

@timer
def slow(): ...              # slow = timer(slow)

square = lambda x: x*x       # anonymous function
sorted(data, key=lambda r: (r.age, r.name))   # multi-key sort
LEGB & global/nonlocal. Assigning to a name inside a function makes it local unless you declare global x (module scope) or nonlocal x (enclosing function). Reading without assigning falls through the LEGB chain.

Classes & OOP

A class bundles state (attributes) with behaviour (methods). Instance methods take self; __init__ is the constructor; dunder methods let your objects behave like built-ins (__len__, __eq__, __repr__, __lt__ for sorting). The four OOP pillars — encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction — show up as conceptual interview questions, so connect each to a concrete Python feature.

python
class Animal:
    species = "unknown"               # class attribute (shared)
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name              # instance attribute
    def speak(self):                  # to be overridden
        raise NotImplementedError
    def __repr__(self):
        return f"Animal({self.name!r})"

class Dog(Animal):                    # inheritance
    species = "dog"
    def speak(self):                  # polymorphism / override
        return "Woof"
    def fetch(self):
        return f"{self.name} fetches"

d = Dog("Rex")
d.speak(); isinstance(d, Animal)      # True
super()      # call parent method inside an override

class Circle:
    def __init__(self, r): self._r = r
    @property                         # computed read-only attribute
    def area(self): return 3.14159 * self._r**2
    @staticmethod
    def unit(): return Circle(1)      # no self/cls
    @classmethod
    def from_diameter(cls, d): return cls(d/2)   # alt constructor
PillarIdeaIn Python
EncapsulationBundle data + methods, hide internals_protected, __private name-mangling, @property
InheritanceReuse & specialize a base classclass Child(Parent), super()
PolymorphismSame call, type-specific behaviourMethod override, duck typing
AbstractionExpose intent, hide complexityabc.ABC, @abstractmethod

Iterators & generators

Iteration is a protocol: an iterable can produce an iterator via iter(), and an iterator yields items via next() until it raises StopIteration. Generators (functions with yield) build iterators lazily — they produce values on demand, so you can stream gigabytes or model infinite sequences in constant memory. This laziness is the key to scalable ETL in pure Python.

python
def countdown(n):
    while n > 0:
        yield n            # pauses here, resumes on next()
        n -= 1

for x in countdown(3): print(x)     # 3 2 1
gen = (line.strip() for line in open("big.csv"))   # streams, O(1) memory

import itertools as it
it.islice(gen, 10)         # take first 10 without loading all
it.chain(a, b)             # concatenate iterables lazily
next(iter(d))              # first key of a dict
Why it matters for data engineering. Reading a file with a generator processes one row at a time instead of loading the whole file — the difference between a pipeline that scales and one that OOMs.

Errors, exceptions & context managers

Python prefers "easier to ask forgiveness than permission" (EAFP): try the operation and catch the specific exception, rather than checking every precondition first. Catch the narrowest exception you can, use finally for cleanup, and prefer with blocks (context managers) so resources like files and locks are released automatically even on error.

python
try:
    val = int(user_input)
except ValueError as e:
    print("not a number:", e)
except (KeyError, IndexError):
    ...
else:
    print("ok:", val)        # runs only if no exception
finally:
    cleanup()                # always runs

raise ValueError("bad input")          # raise your own
assert x > 0, "x must be positive"     # debugging checks

with open("data.csv") as f:            # auto-closes the file
    data = f.read()

from contextlib import contextmanager
@contextmanager
def timer():
    import time; t=time.perf_counter()
    yield
    print(time.perf_counter()-t)

Standard library toolbox

Interviewers love when you reach for the right batteries-included tool instead of reinventing it. These are the modules worth memorizing for data/ML work.

ModuleReach for it when…Key names
collectionsCounting, grouping, queues, default valuesCounter, defaultdict, deque, OrderedDict, namedtuple
itertoolsCombinatorics & lazy iterationproduct, combinations, permutations, groupby, accumulate, chain
heapqPriority queue / top-k / streaming minheappush, heappop, nlargest, nsmallest
bisectKeep a list sorted / binary searchbisect_left, insort
functoolsCaching, partials, reduce, decoratorslru_cache, cache, reduce, partial, wraps
math / statisticsNumeric helpers, quick statsgcd, comb, log, inf; mean, median, stdev
rePattern matching in textfindall, sub, match, search, compile
datetimeDates, durations, parsingdatetime, timedelta, strptime, strftime
json / csvSerialization & flat-file I/Oloads, dumps; reader, DictReader
python
from functools import lru_cache
@lru_cache(maxsize=None)         # memoize -> turns exp. recursion linear
def fib(n): return n if n < 2 else fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)

from collections import deque
q = deque([1,2,3]); q.appendleft(0); q.pop(); q.popleft()  # O(1) both ends

import heapq
heap = []; heapq.heappush(heap, (priority, item))
smallest = heapq.heappop(heap)
heapq.nlargest(3, data, key=len)

import re
re.findall(r"\d+", "a1b22c")     # ['1','22']
re.sub(r"\s+", " ", messy)        # collapse whitespace

Built-in operation complexity (cheat table)

StructureAccess / SearchInsertDeleteNotes
listidx O(1) / search O(n)append O(1)*, insert O(n)pop end O(1), else O(n)contiguous array
dictO(1) avgO(1) avgO(1) avghash table
setO(1) avgO(1) avgO(1) avghash table
dequeends O(1), middle O(n)both ends O(1)both ends O(1)doubly linked
str / tupleidx O(1) / search O(n)immutableimmutableconcat builds new
heapqpeek min O(1)push O(log n)pop O(log n)binary heap on a list

* amortized — occasional resize costs O(n) but averages to O(1).

Interview gotchas & idioms

Mutable defaults persist across calls. Use None sentinels.
[[0]*n]*m makes shared rows. Use a comprehension.
is vs ==: is only for None/singletons.
Float equality: use math.isclose, never ==.
Late-binding closures in loops capture the variable, not its value — bind with a default arg.
Modifying a list while iterating skips elements — iterate a copy or build a new list.
One-liners worth memorizing. sorted(d, key=d.get) (keys by value), max(counts, key=counts.get) (mode), list(zip(*m)) (transpose), {**a, **b} (merge dicts), "".join(reversed(s)) (reverse string), any()/all() for quantifiers, sum(c==t for c in s) (count by predicate).
🗄️

SQL & ETL

SQL is declarative: you describe the result you want and the engine figures out how to get it. The key to fluency is internalizing the logical order of evaluation — it's not the order you write the clauses — because that order explains why WHERE can't see an alias, why HAVING exists, and what a window function can reference. For data engineering, layer on joins, window functions, and the ETL/ELT mindset of moving and shaping data reliably.

⚡ Cheat-sheet — quick recall

  1. Logical order: FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT.
  2. WHERE filters rows pre-grouping; HAVING filters groups after.
  3. SELECT aliases aren't visible to WHERE/GROUP BY (evaluated too late).
  4. INNER drops unmatched rows; LEFT keeps all left rows — wrong join loses data.
  5. COUNT(*) counts rows incl. NULLs; COUNT(col) skips NULLs.
  6. NULL is unknown: = NULL is never true — use IS NULL.
  7. Window: f() OVER (PARTITION BY … ORDER BY …) keeps rows + adds a stat.
  8. ROW_NUMBER/RANK/DENSE_RANK for top-N-per-group and dedup.
  9. Index the columns you filter/join on; a function on an indexed col disables it.
  10. CTEs (WITH) for readability; subqueries for one-offs.

In this chapter

  • Order of execution
  • SELECT & filtering
  • Joins
  • Aggregation & GROUP BY
  • Window functions
  • CTEs & subqueries
  • Conditionals & NULLs
  • Indexes & performance
  • Data modeling
  • ETL vs ELT & pipelines
  • Common query patterns

The logical order of execution

You write SELECT first, but the engine evaluates it nearly last. This single table explains most SQL "why doesn't this work" questions: column aliases defined in SELECT aren't visible to WHERE (it runs earlier), and you filter aggregates with HAVING (after grouping) not WHERE (before).

#ClauseWhat it does
1FROM / JOINChoose & combine source tables
2WHEREFilter individual rows (no aggregates yet)
3GROUP BYCollapse rows into groups
4HAVINGFilter groups (can use aggregates)
5SELECTPick columns, compute expressions & aliases
6DISTINCTRemove duplicate rows
7ORDER BYSort (can use SELECT aliases)
8LIMIT / OFFSETTake a slice of the result
Mnemonic consequence. Want to filter on a computed alias? You can in ORDER BY (step 7) but not in WHERE (step 2) — wrap it in a subquery/CTE or repeat the expression.

SELECT & filtering

sql
SELECT customer_id,
       amount * 1.2 AS amount_with_tax        -- expression + alias
FROM   orders
WHERE  status = 'paid'
  AND  created_at >= '2026-01-01'
  AND  region IN ('EU', 'US')
  AND  email LIKE '%@gmail.com'               -- % = any chars, _ = one
  AND  amount BETWEEN 10 AND 100              -- inclusive
  AND  coupon IS NOT NULL                     -- never use = NULL
ORDER BY amount DESC
LIMIT 20;
NULL is not a value, it's "unknown". x = NULL is never true — use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL. Any arithmetic or comparison with NULL yields NULL, and NOT IN (… NULL …) can silently return no rows.

Joins

A join combines rows from two tables on a matching condition. The join type decides what happens to rows that have no match: INNER keeps only matches, LEFT keeps all left rows (NULLs where the right has no match), RIGHT the mirror, FULL keeps everything. Picking the wrong one — usually INNER when you needed LEFT — silently drops rows and is the most common data-pipeline bug.

JoinKeepsTypical use
INNER JOINOnly rows matching in bothStrict relationships
LEFT JOINAll left rows + matches"Keep all customers, attach orders if any"
RIGHT JOINAll right rows + matchesRare; flip the table order instead
FULL OUTER JOINAll rows from bothReconciling two sources
CROSS JOINCartesian productGenerating combinations
SELF JOINTable joined to itselfHierarchies, employee→manager
sql
SELECT c.name, COUNT(o.id) AS n_orders
FROM   customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id    -- keep customers w/ 0 orders
GROUP BY c.name;

-- Find customers with NO orders (anti-join)
SELECT c.id
FROM   customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
WHERE  o.id IS NULL;                          -- the "missing" pattern

-- Self join: each employee with their manager
SELECT e.name AS emp, m.name AS manager
FROM   employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;
Fan-out trap. Joining to a table with multiple matching rows multiplies your rows, inflating any SUM/COUNT. Aggregate the child table first (in a CTE) before joining, or count DISTINCT.

Aggregation & GROUP BY

Aggregate functions collapse many rows into one summary. GROUP BY defines the buckets; every non-aggregated column in SELECT must appear in GROUP BY. Remember COUNT(*) counts rows (including NULLs) while COUNT(col) skips NULLs, and that HAVING filters the grouped result.

sql
SELECT region,
       COUNT(*)            AS n_orders,
       COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS n_customers,
       SUM(amount)         AS revenue,
       AVG(amount)         AS avg_order,
       MAX(amount)         AS biggest
FROM   orders
WHERE  status = 'paid'                 -- pre-aggregation filter
GROUP BY region
HAVING SUM(amount) > 10000              -- post-aggregation filter
ORDER BY revenue DESC;
FunctionReturnsNULL handling
COUNT(*)number of rowscounts everything
COUNT(col)non-null valuesignores NULLs
SUM / AVGtotal / meanignores NULLs
MIN / MAXextremesignores NULLs
STRING_AGG / ARRAY_AGGconcatenate group valuesper dialect

Window functions

Window functions compute across a set of rows related to the current row without collapsing them — you keep every row and get an aggregate, ranking, or running total beside it. The anatomy is func() OVER (PARTITION BY … ORDER BY …): PARTITION BY resets the calculation per group, ORDER BY defines sequence for rankings and running sums. These are the single highest-leverage SQL topic for analytics interviews.

FunctionGives you
ROW_NUMBER()unique 1,2,3… (ties broken arbitrarily) — great for dedup & "latest per group"
RANK() / DENSE_RANK()ranking with ties (gaps / no gaps)
LAG() / LEAD()previous / next row's value — period-over-period change
SUM() OVER(...)running / cumulative total
NTILE(n)split rows into n buckets (quartiles, deciles)
FIRST_VALUE/LAST_VALUEboundary values of the window
sql
-- Latest order per customer (classic dedup pattern)
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT *,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id
                            ORDER BY created_at DESC) AS rn
  FROM orders
) t
WHERE rn = 1;

-- Month-over-month revenue change
SELECT month, revenue,
       revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS mom_change
FROM monthly;

-- Running total & 7-day moving average
SELECT day, amount,
       SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY day) AS running_total,
       AVG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY day
                         ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS ma7
FROM daily;
Aggregate vs window. GROUP BY returns one row per group; the same function OVER() returns one row per input row with the group stat attached. Use windows when you need detail and summary together.

CTEs & subqueries

A Common Table Expression (WITH … AS) names a query so you can build a pipeline of readable steps instead of nesting subqueries inward. CTEs make complex logic legible and let you reuse a result; recursive CTEs walk hierarchies and generate sequences. Subqueries still shine for quick scalar lookups and EXISTS checks.

sql
WITH paid AS (
    SELECT customer_id, amount FROM orders WHERE status = 'paid'
),
totals AS (
    SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS spend FROM paid GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT c.name, t.spend
FROM   totals t
JOIN   customers c ON c.id = t.customer_id
WHERE  t.spend > 1000;

-- Correlated subquery: customers above their region's average
SELECT * FROM orders o
WHERE amount > (SELECT AVG(amount) FROM orders WHERE region = o.region);

-- EXISTS (often faster than IN for "has any match")
SELECT * FROM customers c
WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id = c.id);

-- Recursive CTE: org hierarchy
WITH RECURSIVE tree AS (
    SELECT id, manager_id, 1 AS lvl FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT e.id, e.manager_id, t.lvl + 1
    FROM employees e JOIN tree t ON e.manager_id = t.id
)
SELECT * FROM tree;

Conditionals, NULLs & type handling

sql
-- CASE: inline if/else, e.g. bucketing
SELECT amount,
       CASE WHEN amount < 50  THEN 'low'
            WHEN amount < 200 THEN 'mid'
            ELSE 'high' END AS tier
FROM orders;

COALESCE(discount, 0)            -- first non-NULL -> default values
NULLIF(a, b)                     -- NULL if a = b (guard divide-by-zero)
CAST(amount AS DECIMAL(10,2))    -- explicit type conversion

-- Conditional aggregation (pivot a column into metrics)
SELECT
  SUM(CASE WHEN status='paid'     THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS paid,
  SUM(CASE WHEN status='refunded' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS refunded
FROM orders;

Indexes & performance

An index is a sorted lookup structure (usually a B-tree) that lets the engine find rows without scanning the whole table — turning an O(n) scan into O(log n). They speed up reads on filtered/joined/sorted columns but cost extra storage and slow writes, so you index the columns you filter and join on, not everything. When a query is slow, read the EXPLAIN plan to see whether it's doing a costly full scan.

LeverWhy it helps
Index WHERE/JOIN/ORDER BY columnsAvoids full table scans
Select only needed columnsLess I/O than SELECT *; enables covering indexes
Filter early, before joins/aggregationShrinks the working set
Avoid functions on indexed columns in WHEREWHERE YEAR(d)=2026 disables the index; use a range
Partition / cluster large tables by dateEngine prunes irrelevant partitions
Read EXPLAIN (ANALYZE)Reveals scans, join order, row estimates
Index trade-off in one line. Indexes make reads faster and writes slower and use disk — index the access paths that matter, measure with EXPLAIN, don't index blindly.

Data modeling

Normalization (OLTP / transactional systems) splits data into many tables to remove redundancy so each fact lives in one place — great for write integrity. Denormalization and the star schema (analytics / warehousing) deliberately duplicate data into wide fact + dimension tables so analysts can answer questions with fewer joins — great for read speed. Knowing why each exists is a standard data-engineering question.

ConceptMeaning
Primary keyUnique row identifier (not NULL)
Foreign keyReference to another table's PK — enforces referential integrity
1NF / 2NF / 3NFAtomic columns / no partial deps / no transitive deps
Fact tableMeasurable events (sales, clicks) — many rows, numeric measures
Dimension tableDescriptive context (date, product, customer)
Star vs snowflakeDenormalized dims vs further-normalized dims
Slowly Changing DimensionHow you track history (Type 1 overwrite, Type 2 versioned rows)

ETL vs ELT & pipeline concepts

Both move data from sources into a warehouse; the difference is when you transform. ETL (Extract → Transform → Load) cleans data before loading — classic for on-prem warehouses with limited compute. ELT (Extract → Load → Transform) lands raw data first and transforms inside a powerful cloud warehouse (Snowflake/BigQuery) using SQL/dbt — the modern default because storage is cheap and compute scales. Beyond order, the engineering is about idempotency, incremental loads, and data quality.

StageWhat happensWatch for
ExtractPull from APIs, DBs, files, streamsRate limits, schema drift, incremental cursors
TransformClean, dedup, join, aggregate, conform typesNULLs, timezones, fan-out, business rules
LoadWrite to warehouse / lakeIdempotency, upserts (MERGE), partitioning
Concepts interviewers probe. Idempotency — rerunning a job produces the same result (use MERGE/upsert, not blind INSERT). Incremental load — only process new/changed rows via a high-water-mark column. Backfill — reprocessing history. Orchestration — DAGs (Airflow/Dagster/Prefect) with dependencies, retries, scheduling. Data quality — null/uniqueness/freshness tests. Batch vs streaming — windowed jobs vs event-by-event (Kafka, Spark Structured Streaming).
sql
-- Idempotent upsert (warehouse MERGE) — safe to re-run
MERGE INTO dim_customer AS t
USING staging_customer AS s   ON t.id = s.id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET name = s.name, updated_at = s.updated_at
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (id, name) VALUES (s.id, s.name);

-- Incremental extract via high-water mark
SELECT * FROM source_events
WHERE updated_at > :last_run_timestamp;     -- only new/changed rows

Common query patterns to memorize

TaskTechnique
Nth highest valueDENSE_RANK() then filter = N, or OFFSET N-1 LIMIT 1
Latest row per groupROW_NUMBER() PARTITION BY group ORDER BY ts DESC, keep rn=1
Find duplicatesGROUP BY cols HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
Rows with no matchLEFT JOIN … WHERE right.id IS NULL
Running totalSUM(x) OVER (ORDER BY d)
Period-over-periodLAG() / LEAD()
Pivot categories to columnsConditional SUM(CASE WHEN …)
Percent of totalx / SUM(x) OVER ()
Gaps & islands / streaksrow-number difference trick
🐼

Pandas

Pandas is the in-memory data workbench between your raw source and your model: extract with SQL, reshape and clean with pandas, then explore and model. This chapter is the why behind the recipes in the Cargo Cookbook — the mental models (index alignment, split-apply-combine, vectorisation) that make the syntax obvious instead of memorised.

⚡ Cheat-sheet — quick recall

  1. A DataFrame is dict-of-Series sharing one index; the index drives everything.
  2. Operations align on labels, not position — mismatched indexes give NaN.
  3. .loc = labels, .iloc = positions; use .loc[mask, cols] to avoid SettingWithCopy.
  4. Vectorise — column math runs in C; .apply / loops are last resorts.
  5. GroupBy = split-apply-combine: agg reduces, transform keeps shape, filter drops groups.
  6. merge = SQL join; set how= deliberately and check cardinality with validate=.
  7. Reshape: pivot_table long→wide, melt wide→long; aim for tidy data.
  8. Right dtypes matter: category for repeated strings, datetime64 for dates.
  9. Missing data propagates as NaN; impute by group, not blindly globally.
  10. Time series need a DatetimeIndex; then resample, rolling, shift just work.

In this chapter

  • Series & DataFrame
  • The index & automatic alignment
  • Selection: loc, iloc & SettingWithCopy
  • Vectorisation & broadcasting
  • Missing data
  • dtypes & memory
  • GroupBy: split–apply–combine
  • Reshaping & tidy data
  • Merge / join semantics
  • Time series
  • Interview gotchas

Series & DataFrame

Pandas has two core objects. A Series is a 1D labelled array (values + an index); a DataFrame is a 2D table that is really a dict of Series all sharing one row index. Almost every pandas behaviour follows from that: columns are Series, and the index is what ties everything together. Holding this model — "a DataFrame is aligned columns" — explains alignment, joins, and group-by later.

python
import pandas as pd
s = pd.Series([671, 1240, 512], index=['AMS','FRA','DXB'], name='weight')
s['FRA']          # label lookup -> 1240
df = pd.DataFrame({'weight': s, 'pieces': [19, 33, 5]})
df['weight']      # a column IS a Series
df.shape, df.columns, df.index
Mental model. Think of a DataFrame as a spreadsheet where every column is a typed Series and every row shares the same index labels. Most "why did this happen" questions resolve to the index.

The index & automatic alignment

The index is not just row numbers — it is a set of labels pandas uses to align data automatically. When you combine two Series/DataFrames, pandas matches on the index labels, not on position, and fills non-overlapping labels with NaN. This is the feature that prevents silent off-by-one bugs, and the one that surprises people coming from NumPy.

python
a = pd.Series([1, 2, 3], index=['x','y','z'])
b = pd.Series([10, 20, 30], index=['z','y','x'])
a + b            # aligns by label: x->31, y->22, z->13 (NOT positional!)

# Reset / set the index when you need a clean integer index or a key column
df = df.reset_index().rename(columns={'index':'origin'})
df = df.set_index('origin')
Why it matters. Assigning a column from another DataFrame aligns on index — if the indexes don't match you get NaNs. Use .values or .reset_index(drop=True) to force positional behaviour deliberately.

Selection: loc, iloc & SettingWithCopy

There are three ways to select, and mixing them is the top source of bugs. [] selects columns (or rows by boolean mask); .loc[] selects by label; .iloc[] selects by integer position. To both filter rows and pick columns in one safe step, use .loc[mask, cols] — chaining df[mask][cols] = … writes to a temporary copy and triggers SettingWithCopyWarning.

python
df.loc[df['weight'] > 500, ['weight','pieces']]   # rows by mask, cols by label
df.iloc[0:5, [0, 2]]                               # first 5 rows, cols 0 & 2
df.at[idx, 'weight']                               # fast single scalar

# Safe in-place edit (no SettingWithCopy):
df.loc[df['weight'] < 0, 'weight'] = np.nan

# .query for readable filters
df.query('weight > 500 and pieces < 30')
Rule of thumb. If you're about to write df[...][...] = ..., stop and use .loc[rows, cols] = ... instead.

Vectorisation & broadcasting

Pandas/NumPy operations run in compiled C over whole arrays, so column-level math is often 10–100× faster than a Python loop and reads better too. "Broadcasting" lets a scalar (or a smaller shape) stretch across an array. Reaching for iterrows() or a Python for loop over rows is almost always the wrong tool.

python
# Vectorised: whole-column arithmetic, no loop
df['yield'] = df['revenue_usd'] / df['weight_kg']      # broadcasts elementwise
df['is_heavy'] = np.where(df['weight_kg'] > 1000, 1, 0)

# Conditional on many cases
df['band'] = pd.cut(df['weight_kg'], [0, 250, 1000, np.inf],
                    labels=['S','M','L'])

# AVOID: row loops (slow, un-pandacademic)
# for i, row in df.iterrows(): ...
Speed ladder. vectorised NumPy > built-in pandas methods > .apply along a column > .apply(axis=1) > iterrows. Climb down only when you must.

Missing data

Missing values are first-class in pandas, represented as NaN (and NaT for dates). They propagate through arithmetic (anything + NaN = NaN) but are skipped by most reductions (sum, mean) by default. The interview-relevant skill is choosing an imputation that respects structure — e.g. a group median, not a blind global one.

python
df.isna().sum()                       # nulls per column
df['weight_kg'].mean()                # skips NaN by default

# Group-aware imputation (better than a global median)
df['weight_kg'] = (df.groupby('cargo_type')['weight_kg']
                     .transform(lambda s: s.fillna(s.median())))

df = df.dropna(subset=['revenue_local'])   # drop rows missing a key field
Watch out. NaN != NaN, so test with .isna(), never == np.nan. count() ignores NaN; len() does not.

dtypes & memory

Every column has a single dtype. Getting it right is both a correctness and a performance issue: strings stored as object are slow and bulky, repeated strings belong in category, and dates must be datetime64 to unlock the .dt accessor. A column that's secretly object when you expect numbers is a classic source of broken aggregations.

python
df.dtypes
df['cargo_type'] = df['cargo_type'].astype('category')   # huge memory win on repeats
df['booking_ts'] = pd.to_datetime(df['booking_ts'])      # enables .dt
df['n'] = pd.to_numeric(df['n'], errors='coerce')        # bad strings -> NaN
df.memory_usage(deep=True)
Why category helps. It stores each label once + small integer codes, shrinking memory and speeding up group-by/joins on that column.

GroupBy: split–apply–combine

GroupBy is the workhorse, and the mental model is three steps: split rows into groups by key, apply a function per group, combine the results. The function determines the output shape: agg reduces each group to one row, transform returns a value per original row (broadcast back — perfect for shares), and filter keeps or drops whole groups.

python
# agg -> one row per group (named output columns)
df.groupby('airline_id').agg(total=('revenue_usd','sum'),
                             n_cust=('customer_id','nunique'))

# transform -> same shape as input (group value on every row)
df['grp_total'] = df.groupby('airline_id')['revenue_usd'].transform('sum')
df['share'] = df['revenue_usd'] / df['grp_total']

# filter -> drop whole groups by a condition
big = df.groupby('airline_id').filter(lambda g: g['revenue_usd'].sum() > 1e5)
agg vs transform. Need a summary table? agg. Need to add a per-row column derived from its group (rank, share, group-mean)? transform.

Reshaping & tidy data

Data comes long (one observation per row) or wide (categories spread across columns). "Tidy" data is long: one variable per column, one observation per row — the shape most pandas/plotting tools expect. pivot_table goes long→wide (and aggregates), melt goes wide→long, and stack/unstack move levels between the index and columns.

python
# long -> wide matrix (and aggregate duplicates)
wide = df.pivot_table(index='airline_id', columns='cargo_type',
                      values='revenue_usd', aggfunc='sum', fill_value=0)

# wide -> long (tidy) for seaborn/plotting
long = wide.reset_index().melt(id_vars='airline_id',
                               var_name='cargo_type', value_name='revenue')

pd.crosstab(df['airline_id'], df['cargo_type'])   # quick frequency matrix
pivot vs pivot_table. pivot needs unique index/column pairs; pivot_table aggregates duplicates (and is what you usually want).

Merge / join semantics

merge is pandas' SQL join. The how= argument decides what happens to non-matching keys — inner keeps only matches, left keeps every left row — and choosing inner when you needed left silently drops rows, the most common pipeline bug. Always think about key cardinality (one-to-one vs one-to-many) so a join doesn't accidentally multiply your rows.

python
m = (df.merge(cust, on='customer_id', how='left')
        .merge(air,  on='airline_id',  how='left'))

# Guard rails
df.merge(cust, on='customer_id', how='left', validate='many_to_one')
df.merge(cust, on='customer_id', how='left', indicator=True)['_merge'].value_counts()

# Time-aware join: most recent rate on/before each event (point-in-time)
pd.merge_asof(df.sort_values('booking_ts'), fx.sort_values('rate_date'),
              left_on='booking_ts', right_on='rate_date',
              by='currency', direction='backward')
concat vs merge. concat stacks frames (by row or column) on the index; merge matches on key columns. Don't reach for concat when you mean a join.

Time series

With a DatetimeIndex, a whole toolkit unlocks: resampling (change frequency, e.g. to daily/monthly totals), rolling windows (moving averages), and label-based time slicing. The key prerequisite is parsing your timestamps to real datetimes and setting them as the index.

python
ts = df.set_index('dep_ts').sort_index()
daily = ts['revenue_usd'].resample('D').sum()    # downsample to daily totals
daily.rolling('7D').mean()                        # 7-day moving average
ts.loc['2024-03']                                 # partial-string slicing
df['dow'] = df['dep_ts'].dt.dayofweek             # .dt accessor for parts
Resample vs groupby. resample('M') is a time-aware groupby that fills in empty periods; a plain groupby(month) won't create rows for months with no data.

Interview gotchas

The mistakes that come up again and again:

TrapDo this instead
Chained assignment df[m][c]=… (SettingWithCopy)df.loc[m, c] = …
Comparing to NaN with ==.isna() / .notna()
Row loops with iterrows()Vectorise, or .apply as a last resort
INNER join silently dropping rowsUse how='left' + indicator=True
Mixed object dtype breaking mathpd.to_numeric(..., errors='coerce')
Forgetting the index aligns operations.reset_index(drop=True) when you want position
inplace=True everywhereReassign (df = df...); chains read better
📊

Visualisation Gallery

A worked example of every common matplotlib and seaborn plot type — code on the left, the rendered output on the right — followed by complex multi-panel compositions. All plots use the airline-cargo frame df (and daily, a resampled revenue series), so you can copy a block and swap in your own columns. Images are embedded, so they display offline.

⚡ Cheat-sheet — quick recall

  1. Pick the chart for the question: distribution→hist/box, relationship→scatter, trend→line.
  2. Default to the explicit fig, ax = plt.subplots() API.
  3. seaborn = statistical defaults; figure-level (relplot/displot/catplot) = facets.
  4. Right-skewed data → log scale or np.log1p.
  5. Always label axes/units/title; add a legend when using hue.
  6. Overplotting → hexbin / 2D-hist / alpha; heatmap for matrices.
  7. Compose dashboards with subplots, GridSpec, subplot_mosaic.
  8. bbox_inches='tight' / tight_layout() stops labels clipping.
Assumed setup for every snippet below:
python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
sns.set_theme(style='whitegrid')

# df = your cleaned cargo table (see the Cargo Cookbook)
# daily = df.set_index('dep_ts').resample('D')['revenue_usd'].sum()

In this chapter

  • Matplotlib plot types
  • Seaborn — relational plots
  • Seaborn — distribution plots
  • Seaborn — categorical plots
  • Seaborn — regression plots
  • Seaborn — matrix & grid plots
  • Complex & multi-panel figures

Matplotlib plot types

The full matplotlib vocabulary — lines, bars, distributions, fields, 3D and more. Each uses the explicit fig, ax = plt.subplots() style you should default to.

Line plot — plt.plot — a value over an ordered axis (trend over time).
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6,3.2))
ax.plot(daily.index, daily.values, color='#b57edc')
ax.set(title='Daily revenue', xlabel='Date', ylabel='USD')
fig.autofmt_xdate()
Multiple lines + legend — Several series on shared axes; label each and call ax.legend().
python
piv = df.pivot_table(index='month', columns='alliance',
                     values='revenue_usd', aggfunc='sum')
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6,3.2))
for col in piv.columns:
    ax.plot(piv.index, piv[col], marker='o', label=col)
ax.set(title='Monthly revenue by alliance', xlabel='Month', ylabel='USD')
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
Scatter / bubble — plt.scatter with size (s) and colour (c) encodes 4 dimensions at once.
python
s = df.sample(200, random_state=1)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6,3.4))
sc = ax.scatter(s['weight_kg'], s['revenue_usd'],
                s=s['pieces']*4, c=s['lead_days'],
                cmap='viridis', alpha=.6, edgecolor='w', linewidth=.3)
ax.set(title='Weight vs revenue (size=pieces, colour=lead days)',
       xlabel='Weight (kg)', ylabel='Revenue (USD)')
fig.colorbar(sc, label='lead days')
Bar chart — plt.bar for categorical magnitudes.
python
g = df.groupby('cargo_type')['revenue_usd'].sum().sort_values()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6,3.3))
ax.bar(g.index, g.values, color='#6dd6c4')
ax.set(title='Revenue by cargo type', ylabel='USD')
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
Horizontal bar — barh — better when category labels are long.
python
g = df.groupby('route')['revenue_usd'].sum().nlargest(10).sort_values()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6,3.6))
ax.barh(g.index, g.values, color='#ff8fb3')
ax.set(title='Top 10 routes by revenue', xlabel='USD')
Grouped bar — Side-by-side bars compare a second category; offset x by a width.
python
piv = df.pivot_table(index='alliance', columns='account_tier',
                     values='revenue_usd', aggfunc='sum', fill_value=0)
x = np.arange(len(piv.index)); w = 0.2
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
for i, tier in enumerate(piv.columns):
    ax.bar(x + i*w, piv[tier], width=w, label=tier)
ax.set_xticks(x + w*1.5); ax.set_xticklabels(piv.index)
ax.set(title='Revenue by alliance & tier', ylabel='USD'); ax.legend(fontsize=7)
Stacked bar — Pass bottom= to stack segments into one bar.
python
piv = df.pivot_table(index='alliance', columns='account_tier',
                     values='revenue_usd', aggfunc='sum', fill_value=0)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
bottom = np.zeros(len(piv))
for tier in piv.columns:
    ax.bar(piv.index, piv[tier], bottom=bottom, label=tier); bottom += piv[tier].values
ax.set(title='Stacked revenue by tier', ylabel='USD'); ax.legend(fontsize=7)
Histogram — plt.hist — distribution of one numeric variable into bins.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6,3.2))
ax.hist(df['weight_kg'], bins=40, color='#b57edc', edgecolor='white')
ax.set(title='Weight distribution', xlabel='Weight (kg)', ylabel='Count')
Pie / donut — Share of a whole; add wedgeprops width for a donut.
python
g = df.groupby('cargo_type')['revenue_usd'].sum()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.6,4.0))
ax.pie(g, labels=g.index, autopct='%1.0f%%', startangle=90,
       wedgeprops=dict(width=0.45), textprops={'fontsize':8})
ax.set(title='Revenue share by cargo type'); ax.axis('equal')
Box plot (matplotlib) — Five-number summary + outliers per group.
python
groups = [g['weight_kg'].values for _, g in df.groupby('cargo_type')]
labels = df['cargo_type'].unique()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
ax.boxplot(groups, labels=sorted(df['cargo_type'].unique()))
ax.set(title='Weight by cargo type', ylabel='kg')
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
Violin plot (matplotlib) — Distribution shape (a smoothed density) per group.
python
groups = [g['yield_usd_kg'].values for _, g in df.groupby('cargo_type')]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
ax.violinplot(groups, showmedians=True)
ax.set_xticks(range(1, len(groups)+1))
ax.set_xticklabels(sorted(df['cargo_type'].unique()), rotation=45)
ax.set(title='Yield distribution by cargo type', ylabel='USD/kg')
Error bars — Mean ± spread; use yerr for uncertainty.
python
stats = df.groupby('cargo_type')['yield_usd_kg'].agg(['mean','std'])
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
ax.errorbar(stats.index, stats['mean'], yerr=stats['std'],
            fmt='o', capsize=4, color='#b57edc', ecolor='#ff8fb3')
ax.set(title='Mean yield ± std', ylabel='USD/kg')
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
Area (fill_between) — Shade between a line and a baseline (or two lines).
python
roll = daily.rolling(7).mean()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6,3.2))
ax.fill_between(roll.index, roll.values, color='#6dd6c4', alpha=.4)
ax.plot(roll.index, roll.values, color='#1f9e8c')
ax.set(title='7-day avg revenue (area)', ylabel='USD'); fig.autofmt_xdate()
Stacked area (stackplot) — Cumulative contribution of several series over time.
python
piv = df.pivot_table(index='month', columns='alliance',
                     values='revenue_usd', aggfunc='sum', fill_value=0)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.2))
ax.stackplot(piv.index, piv.T.values, labels=piv.columns, alpha=.8)
ax.set(title='Revenue composition by alliance', xlabel='Month', ylabel='USD')
ax.legend(fontsize=7, loc='upper left')
Step plot — Values that change at discrete points (counts, states).
python
c = df.groupby('month')['shipment_id'].count()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6,3.0))
ax.step(c.index, c.values, where='mid', color='#b57edc')
ax.set(title='Shipments per month', xlabel='Month', ylabel='Count')
Stem plot — Discrete values as stems — good for sparse sequences.
python
c = df.groupby('month')['revenue_usd'].sum()/1e3
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6,3.0))
ax.stem(c.index, c.values)
ax.set(title='Monthly revenue (k USD)', xlabel='Month', ylabel='kUSD')
Hexbin — Density of a 2D scatter when points overlap heavily.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.4,3.6))
hb = ax.hexbin(df['weight_kg'], df['revenue_usd'], gridsize=25, cmap='magma')
ax.set(title='Weight vs revenue density', xlabel='kg', ylabel='USD')
fig.colorbar(hb, label='count')
2D histogram — hist2d bins two variables into a heat grid.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.4,3.6))
h = ax.hist2d(df['weight_kg'], df['lead_days'], bins=25, cmap='viridis')
ax.set(title='Weight vs lead days', xlabel='kg', ylabel='lead days')
fig.colorbar(h[3], label='count')
imshow (matrix/image) — Render a 2D array as a colour grid.
python
piv = df.pivot_table(index='alliance', columns='cargo_type',
                     values='revenue_usd', aggfunc='sum', fill_value=0)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.4))
im = ax.imshow(piv.values, cmap='YlGnBu', aspect='auto')
ax.set_xticks(range(len(piv.columns))); ax.set_xticklabels(piv.columns, rotation=45, fontsize=7)
ax.set_yticks(range(len(piv.index))); ax.set_yticklabels(piv.index, fontsize=7)
ax.set(title='Revenue matrix'); fig.colorbar(im)
pcolormesh — Like imshow but for explicit (possibly irregular) grids.
python
x = np.linspace(-3,3,60); y = np.linspace(-3,3,60)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y); Z = np.exp(-(X**2 + Y**2))
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.0,3.6))
pc = ax.pcolormesh(X, Y, Z, cmap='plasma', shading='auto')
ax.set(title='2D Gaussian (pcolormesh)'); fig.colorbar(pc)
Contour — contourf fills level sets; contour draws the lines.
python
x = np.linspace(-3,3,80); y = np.linspace(-3,3,80)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y); Z = np.sin(X)*np.cos(Y)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.0,3.6))
cf = ax.contourf(X, Y, Z, levels=12, cmap='RdBu')
ax.contour(X, Y, Z, levels=12, colors='k', linewidths=.3)
ax.set(title='Contour of sin(x)cos(y)'); fig.colorbar(cf)
Quiver (vector field) — Arrows showing direction + magnitude at grid points.
python
x = np.linspace(-2,2,12); y = np.linspace(-2,2,12)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y); U = -Y; V = X
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.6,3.8))
ax.quiver(X, Y, U, V, np.hypot(U,V), cmap='cool')
ax.set(title='Rotational field (quiver)'); ax.set_aspect('equal')
Streamplot — Smooth flow lines through a vector field.
python
x = np.linspace(-3,3,60); y = np.linspace(-3,3,60)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y); U = -1 - X**2 + Y; V = 1 + X - Y**2
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.8,3.8))
ax.streamplot(X, Y, U, V, color=np.hypot(U,V), cmap='autumn', density=1.1)
ax.set(title='Streamplot')
Polar plot — Data on angle/radius axes (cyclical data, radar charts).
python
cats = df.groupby('cargo_type')['revenue_usd'].mean()
ang = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, len(cats), endpoint=False)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.4,4.0), subplot_kw={'projection':'polar'})
ax.bar(ang, cats.values, width=0.5, color=plt.cm.viridis(np.linspace(0,1,len(cats))))
ax.set_xticks(ang); ax.set_xticklabels(cats.index, fontsize=7)
ax.set(title='Mean revenue by cargo type (polar)')
Log scale — Set ax.set_yscale("log") for data spanning orders of magnitude.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6,3.2))
ax.hist(df['revenue_usd'], bins=40, color='#ffc46b', edgecolor='white')
ax.set_yscale('log')
ax.set(title='Revenue (log-count axis)', xlabel='USD', ylabel='log count')
Twin axes (dual y) — twinx() overlays two series with different units/scales.
python
m_rev = df.groupby('month')['revenue_usd'].sum()/1e3
m_cnt = df.groupby('month')['shipment_id'].count()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
ax.bar(m_rev.index, m_rev.values, color='#cdb4f0', label='Revenue')
ax.set_ylabel('Revenue (kUSD)')
ax2 = ax.twinx()
ax2.plot(m_cnt.index, m_cnt.values, color='#ff5d8f', marker='o', label='Shipments')
ax2.set_ylabel('Shipments')
ax.set(title='Revenue (bar) + shipments (line)', xlabel='Month')
Annotations — Text, arrows and shaded spans to call out features.
python
roll = daily.rolling(7).mean()
peak = roll.idxmax()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.2))
ax.plot(roll.index, roll.values, color='#b57edc')
ax.annotate('peak', xy=(peak, roll.max()),
            xytext=(peak, roll.max()*1.15),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->'), fontsize=9)
ax.axvspan(roll.index[20], roll.index[40], color='#ffc46b', alpha=.2)
ax.set(title='Annotated rolling revenue', ylabel='USD'); fig.autofmt_xdate()
eventplot (raster) — Mark event times along rows — timelines, spike trains.
python
data = [df[df.alliance==a]['month'].values for a in df['alliance'].unique()[:4]]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.0))
ax.eventplot(data, colors=['#b57edc','#ff8fb3','#6dd6c4','#ffc46b'])
ax.set_yticks(range(len(data))); ax.set_yticklabels(df['alliance'].unique()[:4], fontsize=7)
ax.set(title='Booking months by alliance (eventplot)', xlabel='Month')
3D surface — mplot3d: a surface z=f(x,y).
python
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D
x = np.linspace(-3,3,50); y = np.linspace(-3,3,50)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y); Z = np.sin(np.sqrt(X**2 + Y**2))
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(5.0,3.8))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z, cmap='viridis')
ax.set(title='3D surface')
3D scatter — Three numeric axes; colour adds a fourth dimension.
python
s = df.sample(150, random_state=2)
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(5.0,3.8))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
p = ax.scatter(s['weight_kg'], s['lead_days'], s['revenue_usd'],
               c=s['revenue_usd'], cmap='plasma', s=14)
ax.set(xlabel='kg', ylabel='lead', zlabel='USD', title='3D scatter')

Seaborn — relational plots

scatter / line relationships, including the figure-level relplot for facets.

scatterplot — Relationship between two numerics; hue/size/style add dimensions.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.6))
sns.scatterplot(data=df.sample(300, random_state=1), x='weight_kg', y='revenue_usd',
                hue='cargo_type', size='pieces', sizes=(15,150), alpha=.6, ax=ax)
ax.legend(fontsize=6, loc='upper left', ncol=2)
ax.set_title('scatterplot with hue + size')
lineplot (with CI) — Aggregates duplicate x values and shades a confidence band.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
sns.lineplot(data=df, x='month', y='yield_usd_kg', hue='alliance',
             errorbar=('ci',95), ax=ax)
ax.legend(fontsize=7); ax.set_title('lineplot: mean yield by month (95% CI)')
relplot (faceted) — Figure-level: small multiples via col=/row=.
python
g = sns.relplot(data=df.sample(400, random_state=1), x='weight_kg', y='revenue_usd',
                col='alliance', hue='cargo_type', col_wrap=3, height=2.3,
                facet_kws={'sharey':True})
g.set_titles('{col_name}')
for ax in g.axes.flat: ax.legend_ = None
g.fig.suptitle('relplot faceted by alliance', y=1.03, fontsize=10)

Seaborn — distribution plots

How one (or two) variables are distributed: histograms, KDEs, ECDFs, rugs.

histplot — Histogram with optional kde and hue overlay.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
sns.histplot(data=df, x='weight_kg', hue='cargo_type', element='step',
             kde=True, ax=ax, legend=True)
ax.set_title('histplot with kde + hue')
kdeplot (1D & 2D) — Smoothed density; pass two vars + fill for a 2D density.
python
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(7.2,3.0))
sns.kdeplot(data=df, x='lead_days', hue='alliance', fill=True, alpha=.3, ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title('1D kde'); axes[0].legend_.set_visible(False)
sns.kdeplot(data=df.sample(500, random_state=1), x='weight_kg', y='revenue_usd',
            fill=True, cmap='mako', ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title('2D kde')
ecdfplot — Empirical CDF — read percentiles directly, no binning choice.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.2))
sns.ecdfplot(data=df, x='revenue_usd', hue='cargo_type', ax=ax)
ax.set_title('ecdfplot of revenue')
displot (faceted) — Figure-level distribution with facets.
python
g = sns.displot(data=df, x='yield_usd_kg', col='account_tier', col_wrap=2,
                hue='account_tier', kde=True, height=2.2, aspect=1.3)
g.set_titles('{col_name}')
g.fig.suptitle('displot faceted by tier', y=1.02, fontsize=10)
rugplot + hist — Rug ticks mark every observation along the axis.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.2))
sns.histplot(df['lead_days'], bins=30, color='#cdb4f0', ax=ax)
sns.rugplot(df['lead_days'], color='#ff5d8f', height=.05, ax=ax)
ax.set_title('histogram + rug')

Seaborn — categorical plots

Compare a numeric across categories — points, boxes, violins, bars, counts.

stripplot — Raw points per category (jittered).
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
sns.stripplot(data=df.sample(400, random_state=1), x='cargo_type', y='yield_usd_kg',
              hue='cargo_type', jitter=.25, alpha=.5, legend=False, ax=ax)
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45); ax.set_title('stripplot')
swarmplot — Non-overlapping points — shows density + every value.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
sns.swarmplot(data=df.sample(250, random_state=3), x='cargo_type', y='lead_days',
              hue='cargo_type', size=3, legend=False, ax=ax)
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45); ax.set_title('swarmplot')
boxplot — Quartiles + outliers; the categorical workhorse.
python
order = df.groupby('cargo_type')['yield_usd_kg'].median().sort_values().index
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='cargo_type', y='yield_usd_kg', order=order, ax=ax)
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45); ax.set_title('boxplot (ordered by median)')
violinplot — Box + mirrored density; split= compares a binary hue.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
sns.violinplot(data=df, x='cargo_type', y='weight_kg', inner='quartile', ax=ax)
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45); ax.set_title('violinplot')
boxenplot — Box plot variant that shows more tail quantiles (large n).
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
sns.boxenplot(data=df, x='cargo_type', y='revenue_usd', ax=ax)
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45); ax.set_title('boxenplot')
barplot — Mean (or any estimator) per category with CI error bars.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
sns.barplot(data=df, x='cargo_type', y='yield_usd_kg', estimator='mean',
            errorbar=('ci',95), ax=ax)
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45); ax.set_title('barplot: mean yield + CI')
countplot — Frequency of each category (a bar of counts).
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
sns.countplot(data=df, x='cargo_type', hue='account_tier', ax=ax)
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45); ax.legend(fontsize=7, title='tier')
ax.set_title('countplot by tier')
pointplot — Estimator + CI as points joined by lines — good for interactions.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8,3.3))
sns.pointplot(data=df, x='account_tier', y='yield_usd_kg', hue='alliance',
              dodge=.3, errorbar=('ci',90), ax=ax)
ax.legend(fontsize=7); ax.set_title('pointplot: yield by tier & alliance')
catplot (faceted) — Figure-level categorical with facets; kind= picks the plot.
python
g = sns.catplot(data=df, x='account_tier', y='revenue_usd', col='alliance',
                kind='box', col_wrap=3, height=2.2)
g.set_titles('{col_name}'); g.set_xticklabels(rotation=45, fontsize=7)
g.fig.suptitle('catplot: revenue by tier, faceted', y=1.03, fontsize=10)

Seaborn — regression plots

Fit and diagnose linear relationships.

regplot — Scatter + fitted regression line with CI.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6,3.4))
sns.regplot(data=df.sample(300, random_state=1), x='weight_kg', y='revenue_usd',
            scatter_kws={'alpha':.3,'s':12}, line_kws={'color':'#ff5d8f'}, ax=ax)
ax.set_title('regplot')
lmplot (faceted) — Figure-level regression with hue/col facets.
python
g = sns.lmplot(data=df.sample(500, random_state=1), x='weight_kg', y='revenue_usd',
               hue='cargo_type', col='cargo_type', col_wrap=3, height=2.2,
               scatter_kws={'alpha':.4,'s':10})
g.set_titles('{col_name}')
g.fig.suptitle('lmplot per cargo type', y=1.03, fontsize=10)
residplot — Residuals vs fitted — a flat cloud means a good linear fit.
python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6,3.3))
sns.residplot(data=df.sample(300, random_state=1), x='weight_kg', y='revenue_usd',
              lowess=True, line_kws={'color':'#ff5d8f'}, ax=ax)
ax.set_title('residplot')

Seaborn — matrix & grid plots

Heatmaps, clustermaps, and multi-panel grids (pairplot, jointplot).

heatmap — Colour-encoded matrix; annot=True writes the values.
python
num = ['weight_kg','volume_m3','pieces','lead_days','revenue_usd','yield_usd_kg']
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6,4.2))
sns.heatmap(df[num].corr(), annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='coolwarm',
            center=0, square=True, cbar_kws={'shrink':.7}, ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Correlation heatmap')
heatmap (triangular mask) — Mask the upper triangle to de-clutter a correlation matrix.
python
num = ['weight_kg','volume_m3','pieces','lead_days','revenue_usd','yield_usd_kg']
corr = df[num].corr(); mask = np.triu(np.ones_like(corr, dtype=bool))
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6,4.2))
sns.heatmap(corr, mask=mask, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='mako',
            square=True, cbar_kws={'shrink':.7}, ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Lower-triangle correlation')
clustermap — Heatmap that reorders rows/cols by hierarchical clustering.
python
piv = df.pivot_table(index='route', columns='cargo_type',
                     values='revenue_usd', aggfunc='sum', fill_value=0)
g = sns.clustermap(piv, cmap='YlGnBu', standard_scale=1, figsize=(5.6,4.4),
                   dendrogram_ratio=.14, cbar_pos=(0.02,.8,.03,.15))
g.fig.suptitle('clustermap: route × cargo', y=1.02, fontsize=10)
pairplot — All pairwise scatters + diagonal distributions.
python
cols = ['weight_kg','revenue_usd','lead_days','yield_usd_kg']
g = sns.pairplot(df.sample(300, random_state=1)[cols + ['cargo_type']],
                 hue='cargo_type', corner=True, height=1.5,
                 plot_kws={'s':10,'alpha':.4})
g.fig.suptitle('pairplot', y=1.02, fontsize=10)
jointplot — One bivariate plot with marginal distributions on the sides.
python
g = sns.jointplot(data=df.sample(400, random_state=1), x='weight_kg', y='revenue_usd',
                  kind='hex', height=4.2, color='#7a4fb0')
g.fig.suptitle('jointplot (hex)', y=1.02, fontsize=10)
JointGrid (custom) — Build a joint plot manually for full control of each panel.
python
g = sns.JointGrid(data=df.sample(400, random_state=1), x='weight_kg', y='revenue_usd', height=4.2)
g.plot_joint(sns.scatterplot, s=12, alpha=.4, color='#b57edc')
g.plot_marginals(sns.boxplot)
g.fig.suptitle('JointGrid: scatter + box margins', y=1.02, fontsize=10)

Complex & multi-panel figures

Composing several plots into one figure: subplot grids, GridSpec, mosaics, dual axes, shared-axis small multiples, and a polished annotated chart.

Subplot grid (2×2) — plt.subplots(2,2) for a compact mixed dashboard.
python
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(7.4,5.0))
axes[0,0].hist(df['weight_kg'], bins=30, color='#b57edc'); axes[0,0].set_title('Weight')
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='cargo_type', y='yield_usd_kg', ax=axes[0,1])
axes[0,1].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45); axes[0,1].set_title('Yield')
axes[1,0].plot(daily.index, daily.rolling(7).mean()); axes[1,0].set_title('Revenue trend')
g = df.groupby('cargo_type')['revenue_usd'].sum().sort_values()
axes[1,1].barh(g.index, g.values, color='#6dd6c4'); axes[1,1].set_title('Revenue by type')
fig.suptitle('2×2 dashboard', fontsize=11); fig.tight_layout()
GridSpec (custom layout) — GridSpec mixes panels of different sizes.
python
import matplotlib.gridspec as gridspec
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(7.6,4.8))
gs = gridspec.GridSpec(2, 3, figure=fig, hspace=.45, wspace=.4)
ax_big = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, :2])
ax_big.plot(daily.index, daily.rolling(7).mean(), color='#b57edc'); ax_big.set_title('Revenue (7d avg)')
ax_pie = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 2])
gg = df.groupby('cargo_type')['revenue_usd'].sum()
ax_pie.pie(gg, autopct='%1.0f%%', textprops={'fontsize':6}); ax_pie.set_title('Mix', fontsize=9)
ax_hist = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 0]); ax_hist.hist(df['lead_days'], bins=20, color='#ffc46b'); ax_hist.set_title('Lead')
ax_box = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 1:])
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='alliance', y='revenue_usd', ax=ax_box); ax_box.set_title('By alliance')
fig.suptitle('GridSpec dashboard', fontsize=11)
subplot_mosaic — Name regions with an ASCII layout — the readable way to compose.
python
fig, axd = plt.subplot_mosaic(
    [['A','A','B'],
     ['C','D','B']], figsize=(7.6,4.6))
axd['A'].plot(daily.index, daily.rolling(7).mean(), color='#b57edc'); axd['A'].set_title('Trend')
sns.histplot(df['weight_kg'], bins=25, ax=axd['B']); axd['B'].set_title('Weight')
sns.countplot(data=df, x='alliance', ax=axd['C']); axd['C'].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45, labelsize=6); axd['C'].set_title('Alliance')
g2 = df.groupby('cargo_type')['revenue_usd'].mean().sort_values()
axd['D'].barh(g2.index, g2.values, color='#6dd6c4'); axd['D'].tick_params(labelsize=6); axd['D'].set_title('Avg rev')
fig.suptitle('subplot_mosaic', fontsize=11); fig.tight_layout()
Shared axes + small multiples — Loop over groups into a row of linked panels.
python
alliances = ['SkyTeam','Star','Oneworld']
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(7.8,2.8), sharey=True)
for ax, a in zip(axes, alliances):
    sub = df[df.alliance==a].groupby('month')['revenue_usd'].sum()
    ax.plot(sub.index, sub.values, marker='o', color='#b57edc')
    ax.set_title(a, fontsize=9); ax.set_xlabel('month')
axes[0].set_ylabel('USD'); fig.suptitle('Small multiples (shared y)', fontsize=11)
fig.tight_layout()
Bar + line combo (dual axis) — Layer a trend line over bars with a second y-axis.
python
m_rev = df.groupby('month')['revenue_usd'].sum()/1e3
m_yld = df.groupby('month')['yield_usd_kg'].mean()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.0,3.4))
ax.bar(m_rev.index, m_rev.values, color='#cdb4f0', alpha=.8, label='Revenue (kUSD)')
ax2 = ax.twinx()
ax2.plot(m_yld.index, m_yld.values, color='#ff5d8f', marker='o', lw=2, label='Avg yield')
ax.set_xlabel('Month'); ax.set_ylabel('Revenue (kUSD)'); ax2.set_ylabel('Yield (USD/kg)')
ax.set_title('Revenue + yield combo')
fig.legend(fontsize=7, loc='upper right', bbox_to_anchor=(.9,.88))
Annotated time series — Trend + mean line + shaded band + callout — a polished final chart.
python
roll = daily.rolling(7).mean()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4,3.4))
ax.plot(daily.index, daily.values, color='#d9c8ef', lw=.8, label='daily')
ax.plot(roll.index, roll.values, color='#7a4fb0', lw=2, label='7-day avg')
ax.axhline(daily.mean(), color='#ff5d8f', ls='--', lw=1, label='mean')
ax.fill_between(roll.index, roll.values, daily.mean(),
                where=(roll.values>=daily.mean()), color='#6dd6c4', alpha=.25)
ax.annotate('above average', xy=(roll.idxmax(), roll.max()),
            xytext=(roll.idxmax(), roll.max()*1.1), fontsize=8,
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->'))
ax.set_title('Daily revenue with rolling mean'); ax.legend(fontsize=7); fig.autofmt_xdate()
📐

Math & Statistics

For DS/ML the math you actually use clusters into four areas: linear algebra (how data and models are represented and multiplied), calculus (how models learn, via gradients), probability (how uncertainty is modelled), and statistics (how we draw conclusions from samples). The goal isn't proof-writing — it's intuition for what each quantity means and why an algorithm does what it does.

⚡ Cheat-sheet — quick recall

  1. A dense layer is y = Wx + b — linear algebra is ML's language.
  2. The gradient points uphill; gradient descent steps the opposite way.
  3. Convex loss → one global min; GD is guaranteed to reach it.
  4. Mean is outlier-sensitive; median/IQR are robust.
  5. Correlation ≠ causation; variance/std measure spread.
  6. CLT: sample means are ~normal for large n, whatever the population.
  7. p-value = P(data this extreme | H₀), not P(H₀ is true).
  8. A 95% CI captures the true parameter 95% of the time under repetition.
  9. Bayes updates a prior with evidence; rare conditions → many false positives.
  10. Probability goes model→data; statistics goes data→model.

In this chapter

  • Linear algebra
  • Calculus & gradients
  • Probability foundations
  • Distributions
  • Descriptive statistics
  • Sampling & the CLT
  • Inference & hypothesis tests
  • Bayes & conditional probability
  • Combinatorics

Linear algebra

Data is a matrix: rows are observations, columns are features. A model often is a matrix multiply followed by a nonlinearity. So the operations that matter are the dot product (similarity/projection), matrix multiplication (applying a linear transform to every row at once), and the decompositions (eigen/SVD) that reveal a matrix's underlying structure — the engine behind PCA.

ObjectMeaning / use
Scalar, vector, matrix, tensor0-D number, 1-D array, 2-D table, n-D generalization
Dot product \(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=\sum a_ib_i\)Similarity/projection; 0 ⇒ orthogonal
Norm \(\lVert \mathbf{x}\rVert_2=\sqrt{\sum x_i^2}\)Vector length; L1/L2 used in regularization
Matrix product \(C=AB\)Compose linear maps; \((m{\times}k)(k{\times}n){=}(m{\times}n)\)
Transpose \(A^\top\), Inverse \(A^{-1}\)Flip axes; undo a transform (if non-singular)
Identity \(I\), rank, determinant"Do nothing"; independent dims; volume scale (0 ⇒ singular)
Eigen \(A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}\)Directions a transform only stretches — basis of PCA
SVD \(A=U\Sigma V^\top\)Universal factorization; dimensionality reduction, recommenders
Why ML cares. A dense neural layer computes \(\mathbf{y}=W\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{b}\) — a matrix-vector product. PCA finds the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix (the directions of maximum variance). Cosine similarity \(\frac{\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}}{\lVert\mathbf{a}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{b}\rVert}\) powers embeddings and search.
python
import numpy as np
A = np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4]])
A @ B            # matrix multiply (NOT A * B, which is elementwise)
A.T              # transpose
np.linalg.inv(A); np.linalg.det(A)
np.dot(a, b); np.linalg.norm(x)
vals, vecs = np.linalg.eig(A)
U, S, Vt = np.linalg.svd(A)

Calculus & gradients

Training a model = minimizing a loss function. The derivative tells you the slope (how loss changes as a weight changes); the gradient generalizes this to many weights as the vector of partial derivatives, pointing in the direction of steepest increase. Gradient descent therefore steps in the opposite direction to go downhill. That single idea — follow the negative gradient — underlies essentially all model fitting.

Update rule, with learning rate \(\eta\):

text
θ_new = θ_old − η · ∇L(θ)

Written with the gradient symbol: \(\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta\,\nabla_\theta L(\theta)\).

IdeaMeaningML relevance
Derivative \(f'(x)\)Instantaneous rate of changeSlope of loss for one weight
Partial \(\partial f/\partial x_i\)Slope holding others fixedOne component of the gradient
Gradient \(\nabla f\)Vector of all partialsDirection of steepest ascent
Chain rule\((f\circ g)' = f'(g)\,g'\)Backpropagation
Learning rate \(\eta\)Step sizeToo big ⇒ diverge; too small ⇒ slow
Convex functionBowl-shaped, one global minGuarantees GD finds the optimum
Backprop in one sentence. It's the chain rule applied layer by layer from the loss backward, reusing each layer's local gradient to compute how every weight should change.

Probability foundations

Probability quantifies uncertainty on a 0–1 scale. The rules you reuse everywhere: probabilities of mutually exclusive events add; independent events multiply; the complement is \(1-P\). The crucial distinction is independent vs mutually exclusive — these are opposites, not synonyms (mutually exclusive events are strongly dependent: if one happens the other can't).

RuleFormulaWhen
Complement\(P(A^c)=1-P(A)\)"at least one" via 1 − none
Addition\(P(A\cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A\cap B)\)"or" (subtract overlap)
Multiplication\(P(A\cap B)=P(A)\,P(B\mid A)\)"and" (sequential)
Independence\(P(A\cap B)=P(A)P(B)\)One doesn't affect the other
Conditional\(P(A\mid B)=\dfrac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)}\)Update given info

Expected value and variance of a random variable:

\(E[X]=\sum_i x_i\,p_i, \qquad \mathrm{Var}(X)=E[(X-\mu)^2]=E[X^2]-\big(E[X]\big)^2, \qquad \sigma=\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(X)}\)

Distributions you must recognize

A distribution describes how probability is spread over outcomes. Recognizing which one fits a scenario lets you pick the right model and test. The split is discrete (countable outcomes) vs continuous (a density over a range).

DistributionModelsKey params / facts
Bernoullisingle yes/no trialmean \(p\), var \(p(1-p)\)
Binomial#successes in \(n\) trialsmean \(np\), var \(np(1-p)\)
Poisson#events per interval (rare)mean = var = \(\lambda\)
Uniformall outcomes equally likelyflat density
Normal (Gaussian)sums/averages of many effects\(\mu,\sigma\); 68-95-99.7 rule
Exponentialtime between Poisson eventsmemoryless; rate \(\lambda\)

Normal density: \(f(x)=\dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}\)

68–95–99.7 rule. For a normal distribution, ~68% of data lies within 1σ of the mean, ~95% within 2σ, ~99.7% within 3σ. A z-score \(z=\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\) says how many standard deviations a value sits from the mean.

Descriptive statistics

Before modelling you summarise. Central tendency (mean, median, mode) says where data sits; spread (variance, std, IQR, range) says how dispersed it is. The mean is sensitive to outliers while the median is robust — which is exactly why skewed data (income, house prices) is usually reported by median.

MeasureFormula / defNote
Mean \(\bar{x}\)\(\frac{1}{n}\sum x_i\)outlier-sensitive
Medianmiddle value (sorted)robust; use when skewed
Modemost frequent valueonly one for categoricals
Variance \(s^2\)\(\frac{1}{n-1}\sum (x_i-\bar{x})^2\)sample uses \(n-1\) (Bessel)
Std dev \(s\)\(\sqrt{s^2}\)same units as data
IQR\(Q_3-Q_1\)outlier = beyond \(1.5\times\)IQR
Covariance / correlation\(\mathrm{Cov}/(\sigma_x\sigma_y)\)\(r\in[-1,1]\), unitless
Skew / kurtosisasymmetry / tailednessshape diagnostics
Correlation ≠ causation. A high \(r\) means two variables move together, not that one causes the other — a confounder or coincidence can produce it. Also, \(r\) only captures linear association.

Sampling & the Central Limit Theorem

We rarely measure a whole population, so we estimate from a sample. The Central Limit Theorem is why statistics works: regardless of the population's shape, the distribution of the sample mean approaches a normal distribution as sample size grows, with standard deviation \(\sigma/\sqrt{n}\) (the standard error). This is what lets us build confidence intervals and run tests even on non-normal data.

Standard error. \(\mathrm{SE}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\). Quadrupling the sample size only halves the error — diminishing returns. A 95% confidence interval is roughly \(\bar{x}\pm 1.96\,\mathrm{SE}\).

Inference & hypothesis testing

Hypothesis testing asks: could the effect I see be just noise? You assume a null hypothesis \(H_0\) (no effect), compute how surprising your data would be if \(H_0\) were true (the p-value), and reject \(H_0\) if that's below a threshold \(\alpha\) (commonly 0.05). The p-value is not the probability the hypothesis is true — it's \(P(\text{data this extreme}\mid H_0)\).

TermMeaning
\(H_0\) / \(H_1\)Null (no effect) / alternative (effect exists)
\(\alpha\)Significance level; tolerated false-positive rate (0.05)
p-value\(P(\text{data} \ge \text{observed} \mid H_0)\); small ⇒ reject \(H_0\)
Type I errorFalse positive — reject a true \(H_0\) (rate \(\alpha\))
Type II errorFalse negative — fail to reject a false \(H_0\) (rate \(\beta\))
Power\(1-\beta\); chance of detecting a real effect
Confidence intervalRange of plausible values for the parameter
QuestionTest
Compare 2 group meanst-test (paired or independent)
Compare 3+ group meansANOVA
Association between categoricalsChi-square (χ²)
Large-sample mean/proportionz-test
Non-normal / ranked dataMann-Whitney, Wilcoxon
A/B test conversiontwo-proportion z-test
Multiple comparisons. Run 20 tests at \(\alpha=0.05\) and you expect one "significant" result by chance. Correct for it (Bonferroni, FDR) when testing many hypotheses.

Bayes & conditional probability

Bayes' theorem updates a belief in light of evidence — the backbone of Naive Bayes classifiers, spam filters, and the famous "how likely is disease given a positive test" puzzle.
